questions.passage:
The modern gallery teaches distance as though distance were respect. A painting receives a pale wall, a generous interval, and enough empty floor to prevent neighboring works from making an argument. Visitors are expected to approach one object at a time and to regard interruption as a failure of attention. This arrangement seems natural only because its history has been forgotten.

In the great public exhibitions of the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, pictures crowded the walls from the dado to the cornice. Large canvases claimed central positions; smaller works filled the remaining fields; frames touched or nearly touched. Contemporary observers complained of fatigue, bad placement, and favoritism, yet they also understood that the display produced comparisons. A portrait competed with the face above it, a landscape borrowed color from the scene beside it, and a history painting risked appearing theatrical near a quiet domestic interior. The wall behaved less like a row of shrines than a public field of judgment.

Later reformers treated density as visual noise. Spacing promised fairness because each work would receive an independent zone. But independence is not neutral. Isolation protects a painting from embarrassing neighbors. It encourages the fiction that quality resides wholly inside the frame and can be apprehended without rivalry, fashion, scale, or institutional placement. The sparse wall removes obvious hierarchy while making curatorial selection itself harder to see. Once only a few works enter the room, exclusion occurs before the visitor arrives.

Dense hanging did not abolish power. Prime positions mattered, famous artists drew crowds, and committees could bury an unpopular canvas near the ceiling. Yet the crowded wall exposed many of these decisions. Placement was visible as placement. Viewers argued about why one work had been elevated and another stranded. In a sparse gallery, hierarchy often appears as serenity. The chosen object occupies empty space that presents privilege as breathing room.

More important, proximity can produce criticism that no label supplies. Two portraits may reveal a shared convention of posture; three seascapes may expose how mechanically painters distribute light; a modest study may puncture the grandeur of the enormous canvas beside it. Such judgments emerge from relations that curators cannot entirely script. The visitor’s eye moves laterally, constructing temporary alliances and hostilities. Attention becomes comparative rather than devotional.

Defenders of sparse display rightly note that crowded rooms exhaust viewers and can overwhelm delicate works. Density should not become another dogma. Scale, medium, and conservation impose real limits. But fatigue is not the worst possible failure of looking. A room can be comfortable because it asks almost nothing beyond recognition. The isolated masterpiece receives admiration before it has survived comparison.

What deserves recovery is not the old salon wall in every detail but its willingness to let works endanger one another. An exhibition should sometimes permit collision, repetition, contradiction, and unequal scale to become visible. Empty space has its uses, but it should not function as automatic absolution. Paintings gain meaning from formal decisions within their frames, yet they also enter worlds of imitation, rivalry, and institutional choice. A display that erases those relations offers calm at the price of criticism.

The crowded wall was accused of making art difficult to see. Often it made artistic claims difficult to preserve. That is precisely why its principle remains valuable. A work granted solitude can appear self-sufficient; a work placed among competitors must declare what kind of achievement it is.
questions.stem: Which statement best captures the passage's central argument?

choices.A: Historical salon displays should be restored without regard to medium or conservation.
choices.B: Sparse galleries are preferable when visitors lack knowledge of artistic conventions.
choices.C: Curatorial hierarchy disappears when paintings receive equal amounts of wall space.
choices.D: Comparative density can expose artistic and institutional claims hidden by isolation.

explanations.A: The author rejects density as a universal rule and acknowledges practical limits involving scale, medium, and conservation.
explanations.B: The passage criticizes comfortable isolation rather than recommending it for unfamiliar viewers.
explanations.C: Sparse selection can hide exclusion and convert privilege into apparent serenity.
explanations.D: Correct. The author values proximity because rivalry and juxtaposition reveal conventions, weaknesses, and curatorial choices that solitary display can conceal.

questions.stem: The discussion of prime positions and paintings placed near the ceiling primarily serves to

choices.A: prove that crowded exhibitions were more corrupt than modern galleries.
choices.B: show that dense arrangements made hierarchy available for public scrutiny.
choices.C: explain why small paintings were considered inferior to large canvases.
choices.D: suggest that visitors ignored works displayed outside central positions.

explanations.A: The author admits power and favoritism without claiming that the old system was uniquely corrupt.
explanations.B: Correct. Visible differences in placement encouraged viewers to notice and dispute the decisions that structured prestige.
explanations.C: Position reflected hierarchy, but the passage does not equate physical size with artistic inferiority.
explanations.D: An unpopular work could be buried, yet the paragraph emphasizes argument about placement rather than universal neglect.

questions.stem: Which claim is most strongly implied by the passage?

choices.A: A calm display can conceal the selection behind it.
choices.B: Visitors make better judgments when they experience visual fatigue.
choices.C: Every artwork depends entirely on neighboring works for its meaning.
choices.D: Curators cannot influence the comparisons visitors make in dense rooms.

explanations.A: Correct. Sparse rooms present a few chosen works as serene and self-sufficient while making prior exclusion less visible.
explanations.B: Fatigue is acknowledged as a genuine problem and is not treated as a source of better judgment in itself.
explanations.C: The author preserves the importance of formal decisions within each frame while adding relational meaning.
explanations.D: Curators shape placement even though viewers can form comparisons that exceed complete curatorial control.

questions.stem: A photography exhibition places several images of the same civic ceremony beside one another. According to the passage, this arrangement is most valuable when it

choices.A: allows each photograph to be viewed without interference from the others.
choices.B: reduces the need for visitors to notice curatorial selection.
choices.C: reveals recurring conventions and differences through direct comparison.
choices.D: recreates the exact visual density of a nineteenth-century salon.

explanations.A: The author values interference when it produces critical comparison rather than isolated devotion.
explanations.B: Juxtaposition can make selection and relation more visible, not less.
explanations.C: Correct. Neighboring images can expose shared poses, framing habits, and divergent choices that remain less visible in isolation.
explanations.D: The principle of productive comparison does not require literal reconstruction of a historical wall.

questions.stem: Which finding would most weaken the author's defense of comparative density?

choices.A: Visitors spend less total time in galleries when many works share the same room.
choices.B: Large paintings continue to receive central positions when galleries use crowded historical arrangements.
choices.C: Sparse exhibitions require curators to exclude more works before visitors enter the gallery.
choices.D: Visitors in dense galleries compare works less and rely more heavily on artists’ reputations.

explanations.A: Reduced viewing time would confirm the passage’s concession that dense rooms can exhaust visitors, but it would not show that comparison itself fails.
explanations.B: Visible differences in placement support the claim that dense arrangements expose hierarchy rather than conceal it.
explanations.C: This supports the author’s criticism that sparse display hides exclusion by moving selection outside the visitor’s view.
explanations.D: Correct. If density discouraged direct comparison and pushed viewers toward established reputation, it would undermine the claim that proximity generates independent criticism of artistic and institutional claims.

questions.stem: In context, allowing works to “endanger one another” means allowing them to

choices.A: suffer physical damage through close installation.
choices.B: expose one another's conventions and weaknesses through proximity.
choices.C: compete for sale in a public commercial market.
choices.D: lose their independent formal qualities within a larger arrangement.

explanations.A: The phrase concerns critical risk rather than physical danger, and conservation limits remain important.
explanations.B: Correct. Juxtaposition can make repetition, theatricality, borrowed color, and inflated grandeur visible by placing works in direct relation.
explanations.C: Commercial sale is not the meaning of the metaphor in the final argument.
explanations.D: The author retains formal meaning within frames and argues that additional relations become visible.
